analogy: Think of "Terms Concepts and Their Use in Sociology" through the chapter's own opening images: 24 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 2 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY I INTRODUCTION The previous chapter introduced us to an idea both about society as well as sociology. We saw that a central task of sociology is to explore the interplay of society and... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Terms Concepts and Their Use in Sociology is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 24 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 2 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY I INTRODUCTION The previous chapter introduced us to an idea both about society as well as sociology.

The text explains that we saw that a central task of sociology is to explore the interplay of society and the individual.

The text explains that we also saw that individuals do not float freely in society but are part of collective bodies like the family, tribe, caste, class, clan, nation.

The text explains that in this chapter, we move further to understand the kinds of groups individuals form, the kinds of unequal orders, stratification systems within which, individuals and groups are placed, the way social control operates, the roles that individuals have and play, and the status they occupy.

The text explains that in other words we start exploring how society itself functions.

The text explains that the question however remains as to why do we need specific terms and concepts to understand this.

The text explains that why does sociology need to have a special set of terms when we use terms like status and roles or social control anyway in our everyday life?

The text explains that for a discipline such as, say, nuclear physics that deals with matters unknown to most people and for which no word exists in common speech, it seems obvious that a discipline must develop a terminology.

The text explains that however, terminology is possibly even more important for sociology, just because its subject matter is familiar and just because words do exist to denote it.

The text explains that we are so well acquainted with the social institutions that surround us that we cannot see them clearly and precisely (Berger 1976:25).

The text explains that for example we may feel that since we live in families we know all about families.

The text explains that this would be conflating or equating sociological knowledge with common sense knowledge or naturalistic explanation, which we have discussed in Chapter 1.

The text explains that we also found in the previous chapter how sociology as a discipline

The text explains that 25 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY has a biography or history.

The text explains that we saw how certain material and intellectual developments shaped the sociological perspective as well as its concerns.

The text explains that likewise sociological concepts too have a story to tell.

The text explains that peer pressure refers to the social pressure exerted by one’s peers on what one ought to do or not.

The text explains that often social stratification is compared to the geological layering of rock in the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the smaller and simpler the Such stories of achievement do exist and are sources of immense inspiration.

The text explains that social control here is directed to restrain deviant behaviour of individuals or groups on the one hand, and on the other, to mitigate tensions and conflicts among individuals and groups to maintain social order and social cohesion.

The text explains that conflict theorists believe that the scarcity and value of resources in society produces conflict as groups struggle to gain access to and control those resources.

The text explains that some of the main sources of identity include gender, nationality or ethnicity, social class.

The text explains that why do we need to use special terms and concepts in sociology?

The text explains that as a member of society you must be interacting with and in different groups.

The text explains that how do you see these groups from a sociological perspective?

The text explains that what have you observed about the stratification system existing in your society?

The text explains that how are individual lives affected by stratification?

The text explains that do you think the modes of social control in different spheres of society are different?

The text explains that identify the different roles and status that you play and are located in.

The text explains that invitation to Sociology : A Humanistic Perspective.

The text explains that 2003. ‘Gender and the Khasi Family Structure : The Meghalaya Succession to Self-Acquired Property Act,1984’, in ed.

The text explains that sociology of Gender The Challenge of Feminist Sociological Knowledge.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 2: Terms Concepts and Their Use in Sociology

cases.name: If
cases.point: If for Karl Marx class and conflict were key concepts to understand society, social solidarity and collective conscience were key terms for Emile Durkheim. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 1)

cases.name: In
cases.point: In the Post-World War II period sociology was greatly influenced by the structural functionalists who found society essentially harmonious. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 1)

cases.name: Likewise
cases.point: Likewise people of different communities in India have over the long anti-colonial struggle developed an identity as a collectivity and group — a nation with a... (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 3)

cases.name: Primary
cases.point: Primary and Secondary Social Groups The groups to which we belong are not all of equal importance to us. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 4)

schemes.name: TERMS
schemes.point: 27 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY A quasi group is an aggregate or combination, which lacks structure or organisation, and whose members may be... (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 3)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The women’s movement brought about the idea of women’s groups and organisation. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 3)

schemes.name: Caste An
schemes.point: Caste An anti – caste movement A caste based political party Class A class based movement A class based political party Women A women’s movement A... (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 3)

schemes.name: Schools
schemes.point: Schools, government offices, hospitals, students’ associations etc. are examples of secondary groups. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 5)

schemes.name: Inequality
schemes.point: Inequality of power and advantage is central for sociology, because of the crucial place of stratification in the organisation of society. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch2 pg 7)

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

options: Many of the concepts reflect the concern of social thinkers to understand and map the social changes that the shift from pre-modern to modern...
For instance sociologists observed that simple, small scale and traditional societies were more marked by close, often face-to- face interaction.
And modern, large scale societies by formal interaction.
They therefore distinguished primary from secondary groups, community from society or association.
